news_summary: Recent advancements highlight effective non-addictive pain treatments, simple lifestyle efforts reducing major disease risks, surprising brain recovery after stroke, and stronger evidence supporting e-cigarettes as superior smoking cessation aids.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes mettent en lumière des traitements antidouleur efficaces et non addictifs, des changements simples du mode de vie permettant de réduire les risques de maladies graves, une capacité étonnante du cerveau à se régénérer après un AVC, ainsi que des preuves plus solides démontrant que les cigarettes électroniques constituent une aide supérieure pour arrêter de fumer.
news_summary_es: Los avances recientes ponen de relieve tratamientos eficaces contra el dolor que no crean adicción, sencillos cambios en el estilo de vida que reducen el riesgo de padecer enfermedades graves, una sorprendente recuperación cerebral tras un ictus y pruebas más sólidas que respaldan que los cigarrillos electrónicos son una ayuda superior para dejar de fumar.

articles.title: This new therapy turns off pain without opioids or addiction
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a new gene therapy that quiets pain at its source in the brain—without the addictive risks of opioids. Using AI to map how pain is processed, they created a targeted “off switch” that mimics morphine’s benefits but skips its dangerous side effects. In early tests, it delivered lasting relief without affecting normal sensations. The discovery could mark a major step toward safer, non-addictive pain treatments.
articles.content:
A new preclinical study has identified a gene therapy that directly targets pain-processing areas in the brain while avoiding the addiction risks linked to narcotic drugs. The findings could offer new hope to more than 50 million Americans living with chronic pain.

Living with chronic pain is often compared to having a radio stuck at full volume, where the noise never fades no matter what you try. Opioids such as morphine can lower that volume, but they also act on other parts of the brain, which can lead to serious side effects and a high risk of addiction.

Researchers say this new gene therapy works more like a precise volume control that turns down only the pain signal while leaving the rest of the brain unaffected. The work, published in Nature, was conducted by scientists from the University of Pennsylvania Perelman School of Medicine and School of Nursing, along with collaborators from Carnegie Mellon University and Stanford University.

"The goal was to reduce pain while lessening or eliminating the risk of addiction and dangerous side effects," said Gregory Corder, PhD, co-senior author and assistant professor of Psychiatry and Neuroscience at Penn. "By targeting the precise brain circuits that morphine acts on, we believe this is a first step in offering new relief for people whose lives are upended by chronic pain."

AI Helps Map Pain Circuits for Safer Treatments

Morphine, a drug derived from opium, is widely used for pain relief but carries a high potential for misuse. Over time, patients often develop tolerance, meaning they need increasingly higher doses to achieve the same level of relief.

To better understand how morphine works, researchers studied brain cells involved in tracking pain signals. Using these insights, they built an artificial intelligence (AI)-powered system in mice that monitors natural behavior, estimates pain levels, and helps determine how much treatment is needed.

This system served as a guide for designing a targeted gene therapy that reproduces morphine's pain-relieving benefits without triggering addiction. The therapy introduces a brain-specific "off switch" for pain. When activated, it reduces pain over a sustained period without interfering with normal sensations or activating reward pathways associated with addiction.

"To our knowledge, this represents the world's first CNS-targeted gene therapy for pain, and a concrete blueprint for non-addictive, circuit-specific pain medicine," Corder said.

Addressing Chronic Pain Without Fueling the Opioid Crisis

The research reflects more than six years of work supported by a National Institutes of Health New Innovator Award, which enabled the team to investigate how chronic pain develops and persists.

The urgency of safer treatments is clear. In 2019, drug use was linked to 600,000 deaths, with 80 percent involving opioids. A 2025 Pew survey found that nearly half of Philadelphians knew someone with opioid use disorder (OUD), and one-third knew someone who had died from an overdose.

At the same time, chronic pain remains a widespread and costly condition, often described as a 'silent epidemic.' It affects about 50 million Americans and leads to more than $635 million in annual costs, including medical expenses and lost productivity from missed work and reduced earnings. If future studies confirm these findings, this new approach could help reduce that burden by offering effective pain relief without the risks tied to opioids.

Next Steps Toward Clinical Trials

The research team is now collaborating with Michael Platt, PhD, the James S. Riepe University Professor, Professor of Neuroscience, Professor of Psychology, to advance the work toward potential clinical trials.

"The journey from discovery to implementation is long, and this represents a strong first step," Platt said. "Speaking both as a scientist and as a family member of people affected by chronic pain, the potential to relieve suffering without fueling the opioid crisis is exciting."

This work was supported by the National Institutes of Health (NIGMS DP2GM140923, NIDA R00DA043609, NIDA R01DA054374, NINDS R01NS130044, NIDA R01DA056599, NIDA R21DA055846, NIDA F31DA062445, NINDS F31NS143421, NIDA F32DA053099, NIDA F32DA055458, NIDA F31DA057795, NINDS F31NS125927, NIDA T32DA028874, NINDS RF1NS126073), the Howard Hughes Medical Institute, the Whitehall Foundation, and the Tito's Love Research Fund.

Some authors are inventors on a provisional patent application through the University of Pennsylvania and Stanford University regarding the custom sequences used to develop, and the applications of, synthetic opioid promoters (patent application number: 63/383,462 'Human and Murine Oprm1 Promoters and Uses Thereof').
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in gene therapy that offers a non-addictive, targeted treatment for chronic pain, addressing a major public health crisis with broad societal impact. It provides detailed context about the therapy's mechanism, its potential to reduce opioid addiction risks, and the scale of the chronic pain problem in the US, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: New gene therapy offers lasting pain relief without addiction risks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Chronic pain affects about 50 million Americans and is often treated with opioids like morphine. While opioids reduce pain, they can cause addiction and other serious side effects. Finding safer pain treatments has been a big challenge.

**What happened** – Scientists from the University of Pennsylvania and other universities created a new gene therapy that targets pain signals directly in the brain. Using artificial intelligence (AI), they mapped how pain is processed and designed a therapy that acts like an “off switch” for pain. Early tests in animals showed it reduces pain for a long time without causing addiction or affecting normal feelings.

**Impact** – This therapy is unique because it focuses only on the brain circuits that cause pain, avoiding the parts involved in addiction. It could help millions of people with chronic pain find relief without the risks tied to opioid drugs. This is especially important because opioid misuse has led to hundreds of thousands of deaths in recent years.

**What’s next step** – The research team is now working toward clinical trials to test this gene therapy in humans. If successful, it could change how chronic pain is treated and reduce the use of addictive painkillers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new gene therapy uses AI to safely turn down pain signals in the brain, offering hope for long-lasting relief without addiction.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle thérapie génique offre un soulagement durable de la douleur sans risque d’addiction
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Environ 50 millions d’Américains souffrent de douleurs chroniques, souvent traitées avec des opioïdes comme la morphine. Ces médicaments soulagent la douleur mais peuvent entraîner une addiction et d’autres effets secondaires graves. Trouver des traitements plus sûrs est un vrai défi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Pennsylvanie et d’autres universités ont créé une nouvelle thérapie génique qui cible directement les signaux de la douleur dans le cerveau. Grâce à l’intelligence artificielle (IA), ils ont cartographié le traitement de la douleur et conçu une thérapie agissant comme un « interrupteur » pour la douleur. Les premiers essais sur des animaux ont montré qu’elle réduit la douleur longtemps sans provoquer d’addiction ni modifier les sensations normales.

**Impact** – Cette thérapie est unique car elle agit uniquement sur les circuits cérébraux responsables de la douleur, sans toucher aux zones liées à l’addiction. Elle pourrait aider des millions de personnes à soulager leurs douleurs chroniques sans les risques liés aux opioïdes. Cela est crucial, car l’abus d’opioïdes a causé des centaines de milliers de décès ces dernières années.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prépare des essais cliniques pour tester cette thérapie chez l’humain. Si les résultats sont positifs, cela pourrait transformer le traitement des douleurs chroniques et réduire l’usage de médicaments addictifs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle thérapie génique utilise l’IA pour réduire en toute sécurité les signaux de douleur dans le cerveau, offrant un espoir de soulagement durable sans addiction.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva terapia génica ofrece alivio duradero del dolor sin riesgos de adicción
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Alrededor de 50 millones de estadounidenses sufren dolor crónico, que a menudo se trata con opioides como la morfina. Estos medicamentos alivian el dolor pero pueden causar adicción y otros efectos secundarios graves. Encontrar tratamientos más seguros ha sido un gran desafío.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Pensilvania y otras universidades desarrollaron una nueva terapia génica que apunta directamente a las señales de dolor en el cerebro. Usando inteligencia artificial (IA), mapearon cómo se procesa el dolor y diseñaron una terapia que actúa como un “interruptor” para el dolor. Las primeras pruebas en animales mostraron que reduce el dolor por mucho tiempo sin causar adicción ni afectar las sensaciones normales.

**Impacto** – Esta terapia es única porque se enfoca solo en los circuitos cerebrales que causan dolor, evitando las áreas relacionadas con la adicción. Podría ayudar a millones de personas con dolor crónico a encontrar alivio sin los riesgos asociados a los opioides. Esto es especialmente importante porque el mal uso de opioides ha causado cientos de miles de muertes en años recientes.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación está trabajando para avanzar hacia ensayos clínicos en humanos. Si tienen éxito, podría cambiar la forma en que se trata el dolor crónico y reducir el uso de analgésicos adictivos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva terapia génica usa IA para reducir de forma segura las señales de dolor en el cerebro, ofreciendo esperanza de alivio duradero sin adicción.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a softly glowing brain-shaped lantern gently dimming a bright, tangled web of red neural pathways symbolizing pain signals, with a delicate, glowing switch embedded in the lantern softly turning down the light on the pain pathways while leaving the surrounding calm blue and beige background undisturbed, conveying precise, non-addictive relief through gene therapy.

articles.title: Just a few minutes of effort could lower your risk of 8 major diseases
articles.summary: Just a few minutes of getting out of breath each day could dramatically cut your risk of major diseases—including heart disease, dementia, and diabetes. A large study of nearly 100,000 people found that it’s not just how much you move, but how intensely you move that matters. Short bursts of vigorous activity—like rushing for a bus or climbing stairs quickly—were linked to striking reductions in disease risk, especially for inflammatory conditions and brain health.
articles.content:
Just a few minutes of vigorous physical activity each day may significantly lower the chances of developing several major diseases, including arthritis, heart disease, and dementia, according to research published today (March 30) in the European Heart Journal.

To explore this connection, researchers analyzed data from nearly 96,000 people. They compared overall physical activity levels with how much of that activity was vigorous, then tracked participants' risk of developing eight major diseases over time.

Even brief moments of higher intensity effort, such as running to catch a bus, were associated with lower risks of both disease and death. The protective effects were especially strong for inflammatory conditions like arthritis, serious cardiovascular problems such as heart attack and stroke, and dementia.

Large Study Tracks Activity and Health Outcomes

The study was led by an international team that included Professor Minxue Shen from the Xiangya School of Public Health at Central South University in Hunan, China. He said: "We know that physical activity reduces the risk of chronic disease and premature death, and there is growing evidence that vigorous activity provides greater health benefits per minute than moderate activity. But questions remain about the importance of intense activity versus total physical activity. For example, if two people do the same total amount of activity, does the person who exercises more vigorously gain greater health benefits? And if someone has limited time, should they focus on exercising harder rather than longer?"

Participants, all part of the UK Biobank study, wore wrist-based accelerometers for one week. These devices captured detailed movement patterns, including short bursts of vigorous activity that people might not remember or report. Researchers used this data to calculate both total activity and the share that was intense enough to cause breathlessness.

The team then compared these measurements with participants' likelihood of dying or developing eight serious conditions over the next seven years (major cardiovascular disease, irregular heartbeat, type 2 diabetes, immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, liver disease, chronic respiratory diseases, chronic kidney disease and dementia).

Higher Intensity Activity Shows Stronger Benefits

The results showed that people who devoted a larger portion of their activity to vigorous movement had much lower risks across all conditions studied. Compared with those who did no vigorous activity, individuals with the highest levels saw a 63% lower risk of dementia, a 60% lower risk of type 2 diabetes, and a 46% lower risk of death. These benefits were observed even when the total time spent on vigorous activity was relatively small.

The researchers also found that intensity played a larger role for certain diseases. For inflammatory conditions such as arthritis and psoriasis, intensity appeared to be the key factor in lowering risk. In contrast, for diseases like diabetes and chronic liver disease, both how long people were active and how hard they exercised were important.

Why Vigorous Activity Has Unique Effects

Professor Shen said: "Vigorous physical activity appears to trigger specific responses in the body that lower-intensity activity cannot fully replicate. During vigorous physical activity -- the kind that makes you feel out of breath -- your body responds in powerful ways. Your heart pumps more efficiently, your blood vessels become more flexible, and your body improves its ability to use oxygen.

"Vigorous activity also appears to reduce inflammation. This may help explain why we saw strong associations with inflammatory conditions such as psoriasis and arthritis. It may also stimulate chemicals in the brain that help keep brain cells healthy, which could help explain the lower risk of dementia."

Small Changes in Daily Life Can Make a Difference

"Our findings suggest that making some of your physical activity vigorous can provide substantial health benefits. This doesn't require going to the gym. Adding short bursts of activity that make you slightly breathless into daily life, like taking the stairs quickly, walking fast between errands or playing actively with children, can make a real difference. Even 15 to 20 minutes per week of this kind of effort -- just a few minutes a day -- was linked to meaningful health benefits.

"Current guidelines generally focus on the amount of time spent being active per week. Our findings suggest that the composition of that activity matters, and matters differently depending on which diseases you're trying to prevent. This could open the door to more personalized physical activity recommendations based on an individual's specific health risks.

"Vigorous activity may not be safe for everyone, especially older adults or people with certain medical conditions. For them, any increase in movement is still beneficial, and activity should be tailored to the individual."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large, well-conducted study showing that brief daily vigorous physical activity significantly reduces the risk of multiple major diseases and death, providing actionable, broadly relevant health insights that can improve public well-being.
articles.personality_title: Just a few minutes of intense activity daily can cut risk of major diseases
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A large study looked at how physical activity affects the risk of developing serious diseases like heart problems, diabetes, and dementia. Researchers wanted to know if doing more intense exercise, even for a short time, made a difference compared to just moving more overall.

**What happened** – Nearly 96,000 people wore devices on their wrists to track their movements for a week. Scientists measured both total physical activity and how much of it was vigorous—enough to make someone breathe harder. They then followed these people for seven years to see who developed eight major diseases or died.

**Impact** – The study found that even small amounts of vigorous activity, like rushing to catch a bus or climbing stairs quickly, lowered the risk of diseases and death. For example, people who did the most intense activity had a 63% lower chance of dementia and a 60% lower chance of type 2 diabetes. This shows that how hard you exercise can be more important than just how long you move.

**What’s next step** – These findings may help create better exercise advice tailored to people’s health needs. Instead of focusing only on total exercise time, doctors could suggest adding short bursts of intense activity to daily life. However, people with health problems should talk to a doctor before trying harder exercise.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Short bursts of vigorous activity each day can greatly reduce the risk of several serious diseases, even if total exercise time is limited.
articles.personality_title_fr: Quelques minutes d’activité intense par jour réduisent le risque de maladies majeures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une grande étude a examiné comment l’activité physique influence le risque de développer des maladies graves comme les problèmes cardiaques, le diabète et la démence. Les chercheurs voulaient savoir si faire de l’exercice plus intense, même brièvement, faisait une différence par rapport à un simple mouvement prolongé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Près de 96 000 personnes ont porté des appareils au poignet pour suivre leurs mouvements pendant une semaine. Les scientifiques ont mesuré à la fois l’activité physique totale et la part d’activité vigoureuse — suffisante pour faire respirer plus fort. Ils ont ensuite suivi ces personnes pendant sept ans pour voir qui développait huit maladies majeures ou décédait.

**Impact** – L’étude a montré que même de petites périodes d’activité vigoureuse, comme courir pour attraper un bus ou monter rapidement des escaliers, réduisaient le risque de maladies et de décès. Par exemple, les personnes les plus actives intensément avaient 63 % moins de risques de démence et 60 % moins de risques de diabète de type 2. Cela montre que l’intensité de l’exercice peut être plus importante que la durée totale du mouvement.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces résultats pourraient aider à créer des conseils d’exercice mieux adaptés aux besoins de santé des gens. Au lieu de se concentrer uniquement sur le temps total d’exercice, les médecins pourraient recommander d’ajouter de courts pics d’activité intense dans la vie quotidienne. Cependant, les personnes ayant des problèmes de santé devraient consulter un médecin avant d’essayer un exercice plus intense.

**En une phrase** – De courtes périodes d’activité intense chaque jour peuvent réduire fortement le risque de plusieurs maladies graves, même si le temps total d’exercice est limité.
articles.personality_title_es: Solo unos minutos de actividad intensa diaria pueden reducir el riesgo de enfermedades graves
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un gran estudio investigó cómo la actividad física afecta el riesgo de desarrollar enfermedades graves como problemas cardíacos, diabetes y demencia. Los investigadores querían saber si hacer ejercicio más intenso, aunque sea por poco tiempo, marcaba la diferencia frente a simplemente moverse más.

**Qué pasó** – Casi 96,000 personas usaron dispositivos en la muñeca para registrar sus movimientos durante una semana. Los científicos midieron tanto la actividad física total como la cantidad de actividad vigorosa —la que hace respirar más fuerte. Luego siguieron a estas personas durante siete años para ver quién desarrollaba ocho enfermedades graves o moría.

**Impacto** – El estudio encontró que incluso pequeños momentos de actividad vigorosa, como correr para alcanzar un autobús o subir escaleras rápido, reducían el riesgo de enfermedades y muerte. Por ejemplo, quienes hicieron más actividad intensa tuvieron un 63% menos de riesgo de demencia y un 60% menos de riesgo de diabetes tipo 2. Esto muestra que la intensidad del ejercicio puede ser más importante que solo el tiempo total de movimiento.

**Próximo paso** – Estos hallazgos pueden ayudar a crear recomendaciones de ejercicio más personalizadas según las necesidades de salud. En lugar de enfocarse solo en el tiempo total de actividad, los médicos podrían sugerir añadir ráfagas cortas de actividad intensa en la vida diaria. Sin embargo, las personas con problemas de salud deben consultar a un médico antes de intentar ejercicios más fuertes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cortos periodos de actividad vigorosa cada día pueden reducir mucho el riesgo de varias enfermedades graves, incluso si el tiempo total de ejercicio es limitado.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a winding path with symbolic footprints transitioning from gentle walking to dynamic running strides, surrounded by softly glowing icons representing a healthy heart, a flexible blood vessel, and a bright, active brain, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft greens.

articles.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
articles.summary: After a stroke, the brain may do something surprisingly hopeful—it can “refresh” parts of itself. Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.
articles.content:
A new study in The Lancet Digital Health suggests the brain can respond to stroke in a surprising way. Researchers at the USC Mark and Mary Stevens Neuroimaging and Informatics Institute (Stevens INI) found that people with severe physical impairments after a stroke may show signs of a "younger" brain structure in areas that were not damaged. This appears to reflect how the brain adapts and reorganizes itself after injury.

The research was conducted as part of the Enhancing NeuroImaging Genetics through Meta-Analysis (ENIGMA) Stroke Recovery Working Group. Scientists analyzed brain scans from more than 500 stroke survivors collected across 34 research centers in eight countries. By applying deep learning models trained on tens of thousands of MRI scans, the team estimated the "brain age" of different regions in each hemisphere and examined how stroke affects both structure and recovery.

"We found that larger strokes accelerate aging in the damaged hemisphere but paradoxically make the opposite side of the brain appear younger," said Hosung Kim, PhD, associate professor of research neurology at the Keck School of Medicine of USC and co-senior author of the study. "This pattern suggests the brain may be reorganizing itself, essentially rejuvenating undamaged networks to compensate for lost function."

AI Reveals Brain Rewiring After Stroke

To carry out the analysis, researchers used a type of artificial intelligence called a graph convolutional network. This system estimated the biological age of 18 brain regions based on MRI data. They then compared this predicted age with each person's actual age, a measure known as the brain-predicted age difference (brain-PAD), which serves as an indicator of brain health.

When these brain age measurements were compared with motor function scores, a clear pattern emerged. Stroke survivors with severe movement impairments, even after more than 6 months of rehabilitation, showed younger-than-expected brain age in regions opposite the site of injury. This effect was especially strong in the frontoparietal network, which plays an important role in movement planning, attention, and coordination.

"These findings suggest that when stroke damage leads to greater movement loss, undamaged regions on the opposite side of the brain may adapt to help compensate," Kim explained. "We saw this in the contralesional frontoparietal network, which showed a more 'youthful' pattern and is known to support motor planning, attention, and coordination. Rather than indicating full recovery of movement, this pattern may reflect the brain's attempt to adjust when the damaged motor system can no longer function normally. This gives us a new way to see neuroplasticity that traditional imaging could not capture."

Large-Scale Data Reveals Hidden Patterns

The study relied on ENIGMA, a global collaboration that combines data from more than 50 countries to better understand the brain across different conditions. By standardizing MRI data and clinical information from many research groups, the team created the largest stroke neuroimaging dataset of its kind.

"By pooling data from hundreds of stroke survivors worldwide and applying cutting-edge AI, we can detect subtle patterns of brain reorganization that would be invisible in smaller studies. These findings of regionally differential brain aging in chronic stroke could eventually guide personalized rehabilitation strategies," said Arthur W. Toga, PhD, director of the Stevens INI and Provost Professor at USC.

Toward Personalized Stroke Recovery

The researchers plan to continue this work by following patients over time, from the early stages after a stroke through long-term recovery. Tracking how brain aging patterns and structural changes evolve could help doctors tailor treatments to each person's unique recovery process, with the goal of improving outcomes and quality of life.

Learn more about associations between contralesional neuroplasticity and motor impairment by viewing this video made by the Stevens INI.

The study, "Deep learning prediction of MRI-based regional brain age reveals contralesional neuroplasticity associated with severe motor impairment in chronic stroke: A worldwide ENIGMA study," was funded by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) grant R01 NS115845 and supported by international collaborators from institutions including the University of British Columbia, Monash University, Emory University, and the University of Oslo.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the brain's ability to reorganize and 'rejuvenate' undamaged regions after stroke, based on a large-scale international study using advanced AI. This finding has broad implications for understanding neuroplasticity and improving personalized stroke rehabilitation, thus offering hope and tangible benefits to many stroke survivors worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Stroke triggers surprising brain changes that help recovery
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When someone has a stroke, part of their brain gets damaged. Scientists have long studied how the brain heals after a stroke, but many details are still unknown.

**What happened** – Researchers from many countries studied brain scans of over 500 stroke survivors. Using advanced artificial intelligence (AI), they found that while the damaged side of the brain looks older, the opposite side actually appears younger. This younger look shows the brain is trying to rewire itself to make up for the damage.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows the brain can adapt in unexpected ways. The side of the brain not affected by the stroke may become stronger and more active, especially in areas that control movement and coordination. This helps explain how some stroke survivors regain skills despite serious injury.

**What's next step** – Scientists will follow stroke survivors over time to see how these brain changes develop. This could help doctors create better, personalized rehab plans that match each person’s brain recovery, improving their chances of getting better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After a stroke, the brain can renew healthy areas on the opposite side to help people recover lost movement and function.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un AVC déclenche des changements cérébraux surprenants qui aident à la récupération
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsqu'une personne fait un AVC, une partie de son cerveau est endommagée. Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps comment le cerveau guérit après un AVC, mais beaucoup de détails restent inconnus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de plusieurs pays ont étudié les images cérébrales de plus de 500 survivants d’AVC. Grâce à une intelligence artificielle avancée, ils ont découvert que si le côté endommagé du cerveau paraît plus vieux, le côté opposé semble en fait plus jeune. Ce « rajeunissement » montre que le cerveau essaie de se réorganiser pour compenser les dégâts.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que le cerveau peut s’adapter de façon inattendue. Le côté non touché par l’AVC peut devenir plus fort et plus actif, surtout dans les zones qui contrôlent le mouvement et la coordination. Cela aide à comprendre comment certains patients récupèrent des capacités malgré des blessures graves.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont suivre ces patients sur une longue période pour voir comment ces changements cérébraux évoluent. Cela pourrait aider les médecins à créer des plans de rééducation personnalisés, adaptés à la récupération de chaque cerveau, pour améliorer les chances de guérison.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Après un AVC, le cerveau peut renouveler les zones saines du côté opposé pour aider à récupérer les mouvements et fonctions perdus.
articles.personality_title_es: El ictus provoca cambios cerebrales sorprendentes que ayudan a la recuperación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando alguien sufre un ictus, una parte de su cerebro resulta dañada. Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo se recupera el cerebro después de un ictus, pero aún quedan muchos detalles por descubrir.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de varios países analizaron escáneres cerebrales de más de 500 personas que sobrevivieron a un ictus. Usando inteligencia artificial avanzada, encontraron que mientras el lado afectado del cerebro parece más viejo, el lado opuesto se ve más joven. Esto muestra que el cerebro intenta reorganizarse para compensar el daño.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque demuestra que el cerebro puede adaptarse de formas inesperadas. El lado no dañado puede volverse más fuerte y activo, especialmente en áreas que controlan el movimiento y la coordinación. Esto ayuda a entender cómo algunas personas recuperan habilidades pese a lesiones graves.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán a los sobrevivientes de ictus con el tiempo para observar cómo cambian estas áreas del cerebro. Esto podría ayudar a los médicos a diseñar planes de rehabilitación personalizados que se ajusten a la recuperación de cada persona, mejorando sus posibilidades de mejora.

**Resumen en una frase** – Después de un ictus, el cerebro puede renovar las áreas sanas del lado opuesto para ayudar a recuperar el movimiento y las funciones perdidas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two interconnected brain hemispheres depicted as glowing, gently pulsing networks of delicate golden threads and youthful green vines, with one side showing subtle signs of wear and aging while the opposite side radiates vibrant growth and renewal, symbolizing the brain’s adaptive rejuvenation and neuroplasticity after stroke.

articles.title: Scientists say the evidence is clear: E-cigarettes beat patches and gum in helping smokers quit
articles.summary: Nicotine e-cigarettes may be one of the most effective tools yet for quitting smoking, according to a sweeping review of global research. By analyzing findings from 14 major reviews spanning nearly a decade, researchers found consistent, high-quality evidence showing that nicotine vapes outperform traditional methods like patches, gum, and even behavioral support. While some lower-quality studies produced mixed results, the strongest data clearly favored nicotine e-cigarettes.
articles.content:
A comprehensive review of global research on smoking cessation suggests that e-cigarettes containing nicotine may help more people quit smoking than other commonly used methods. These include nicotine replacement therapy (nicotine patches, gum, lozenges, etc.) as well as non-nicotine e-cigarettes and behavioral support programs.

The analysis brings together findings from multiple systematic reviews to provide a clearer picture of what works best for quitting smoking. In total, researchers combined results from fourteen systematic reviews conducted between 2014 and 2023, making the evidence easier to interpret and compare.

Strongest Evidence Favors Nicotine E-Cigarettes

When focusing on the highest-quality studies, the results consistently show that nicotine e-cigarettes lead to higher quit rates than other approaches. In contrast, lower-quality studies produced less consistent and more uncertain findings. Overall, the most reliable evidence points to nicotine e-cigarettes outperforming nicotine replacement therapy, non-nicotine e-cigarettes, and other alternatives.

As part of the project, researchers developed an 'Evidence and Gap Map' (EGM) to highlight areas where more research is needed. Currently, there are no high-quality systematic reviews directly comparing nicotine e-cigarettes with treatments such as cytisine, bupropion, or nicotine pouches. Evidence comparing e-cigarettes with varenicline is also very limited, based on just one small study with a high risk of bias.

The EGM also indicates that evidence on serious adverse events linked to e-cigarettes remains uncertain. In addition, most of the available data comes from high-income countries. Researchers emphasize the need for future studies to better track potential risks and to include populations from low-and middle-income countries.

Researchers Say Evidence Is Clear

Lead author Dr. Angela Difeng Wu, Senior Researcher and Lecturer at the Nuffield Department of Primary Care Health Sciences, University of Oxford, says "We hope this overview and Evidence and Gap Map can lay to rest some claims that evidence is 'mixed' regarding the impacts of nicotine e-cigarettes on smoking abstinence. In fact, the evidence is clear and consistent across all of the meta-analyses we consulted: e-cigarettes are effective at helping people stop smoking."

This research work was funded by Cancer Research UK, Grant Number PRCPJT‐Nov22/100012.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a comprehensive, high-quality scientific review showing that nicotine e-cigarettes are more effective than traditional methods in helping people quit smoking, which has significant positive health implications for a broad population. The research consolidates global evidence, providing clarity on an important public health issue and potentially guiding better smoking cessation strategies worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Nicotine e-cigarettes shown to help more smokers quit than patches or gum
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people try to quit smoking using products like nicotine patches or gum, but success rates vary. Scientists have studied different tools to find out which work best to help smokers stop.

**What happened** – Researchers reviewed 14 major studies from 2014 to 2023 that looked at ways to quit smoking. They found strong, clear evidence that nicotine e-cigarettes help more people quit than traditional methods like patches, gum, or behavioral programs. The review combined results from many studies to compare these methods accurately.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows nicotine e-cigarettes could be a better option for smokers wanting to quit. The research is based on high-quality studies, making the results trustworthy. It also points out that some other treatments haven’t been compared enough yet, and that more research is needed on possible risks and on people in different countries.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to study more about how e-cigarettes compare with other medicines for quitting smoking. They also aim to understand any health risks better and include more diverse groups of people in future research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Strong evidence now shows that nicotine e-cigarettes help more smokers quit than patches, gum, or support programs, offering a promising tool for quitting smoking worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les cigarettes électroniques à la nicotine aident plus à arrêter de fumer que les patchs ou la gomme
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes essaient d’arrêter de fumer avec des patchs ou de la gomme à la nicotine, mais les résultats ne sont pas toujours bons. Des scientifiques ont étudié différents moyens pour savoir lesquels fonctionnent le mieux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont analysé 14 grandes études de 2014 à 2023 sur les méthodes pour arrêter de fumer. Ils ont trouvé des preuves solides que les cigarettes électroniques à la nicotine aident plus de personnes à arrêter que les patchs, la gomme ou les programmes de soutien. Cette analyse regroupe plusieurs études pour comparer ces méthodes de façon fiable.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que les cigarettes électroniques à la nicotine pourraient être une meilleure option pour ceux qui veulent arrêter de fumer. Les résultats reposent sur des études de haute qualité, ce qui les rend fiables. L’étude souligne aussi que d’autres traitements doivent encore être mieux étudiés, notamment leurs risques et leur efficacité dans différents pays.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les scientifiques veulent comparer davantage les cigarettes électroniques à d’autres médicaments pour arrêter de fumer. Ils souhaitent aussi mieux comprendre les éventuels risques pour la santé et inclure plus de populations diverses dans leurs recherches.

**Phrase clé** – Des preuves solides montrent désormais que les cigarettes électroniques à la nicotine aident plus à arrêter de fumer que les patchs, la gomme ou les programmes de soutien, offrant un outil prometteur pour lutter contre le tabagisme dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Los cigarrillos electrónicos con nicotina ayudan a más personas a dejar de fumar que los parches o chicles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas intentan dejar de fumar usando parches o chicles de nicotina, pero no siempre tienen éxito. Científicos han estudiado diferentes métodos para descubrir cuáles funcionan mejor.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores revisaron 14 estudios importantes de 2014 a 2023 sobre cómo dejar de fumar. Encontraron pruebas claras y fuertes de que los cigarrillos electrónicos con nicotina ayudan a más personas a dejar de fumar que los parches, chicles o programas de apoyo. Este análisis reunió muchos estudios para hacer comparaciones precisas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que los cigarrillos electrónicos con nicotina podrían ser una mejor opción para quienes quieren dejar de fumar. La investigación se basa en estudios de alta calidad, por lo que los resultados son confiables. También señala que otros tratamientos necesitan más estudio, especialmente sobre riesgos y en diferentes países.

**Próximos pasos** – Los científicos quieren investigar más cómo se comparan los cigarrillos electrónicos con otros medicamentos para dejar de fumar. También buscan entender mejor los riesgos para la salud y estudiar a más grupos diversos de personas.

**Frase clave** – La evidencia fuerte muestra que los cigarrillos electrónicos con nicotina ayudan a más fumadores a dejar de fumar que los parches, chicles o programas de apoyo, ofreciendo una herramienta prometedora para dejar el tabaco en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting showing a glowing, stylized e-cigarette gently eclipsing and replacing a fading nicotine patch and gum, set against a soft, simple background of calming earth tones, symbolizing the clear and positive evidence that nicotine e-cigarettes help smokers quit more effectively than traditional methods.
